description: A14FJ - Day 6, part 3: Spotting Antares and Going for Docking

tokens: Antares has lifted off from Fra Mauro and is now coasting in lunar orbit.
For the first time, they are performing a direct rendezvous which sees them bypass some of the manoeuvres that had previously been used to ensure that the two spacecraft can meet up in lunar orbit.

Increasing confidence in their navigational abilities has made this new mode of rendezvous a possibility, saving an extra two-hour orbit; another sign of the ever developing nature of Apollo operations.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Editor's note:All transcript times are presented according to the GET update at 054:53:36 that saw the mission timer moved forward 40 minutes, 2.90 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 143 hours, 33 minutes and we're about 8½ minutes now from reacquiring the LM and CSM as they come around on the front side of the Moon.
The Lunar Module should have performed the terminal phase initiation manuever on the backside of the Moon.
This manuever is - a fine computation for it is done onboard.
The figures given from the ground are used as a check and a backup for the planned maneuver.
It looked as if the burn would have probably come out to around to around 85 feet per second and would have been performed at about 143 hours, 10 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
When we reacquire, in about 7 minutes 45 seconds from now, Antares should be on its final approach into the CSM on align aside approach, and nearing the braking gates, beginning to slow down.
The final docking of course, as on previous missions, will be performed by the CSM, by Stu Roosa.
That is primarily because of the visibility difficulties that the crew in the Lunar Module has when the 2 vehicles close at very close rates.
And the plan is for Roosa to proceed with a normal docking.

If, at the time that the probe is inserted into the drogue of the Lunar Module, we don't get an immediate capture, Roosa will deactivate his stabilization control system from the Command Module and the Lunar Module will thrust to hold the probe firmly into the drogue in the hope that if the capture latches on the probe are a bit slow engaging that they will occur at this time.
We would expect that we would get a normal docking, based on the data that has been analyzed from the previous successful docking following translunar injection.

If, however, we do not get a normal docking, we're prepared with several contingency procedures, which range from attempting to energize the neumatic system that retracts the drogue or the probe rather, and latching up with the primary docking latches.

If this doesn't work, we would be prepared to instruct the crew in jumpering an electrical connection so that they would put electrical power onto the activate switch, a control for the probe assembly, hoping to retract it that way.
And the - of course the final procedure would be to go extra vehicular or an extra vehicular transfer.
We're now 5 minutes 30 seconds from reacquiring.

We would like to mention, also at this time that Apollo 12 Commander Charles Conrad will meet with Newsmen in the main auditorium of the MSC News Center at 3:30 pm today to discuss Lunar Surface Operations on Apollo 14.
That will be Astronaut Pete Conrad, in the MSC News Center main auditorium at 3:30 pm today.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 143 hours 41 minutes and we're now less then 45 seconds from reacquiring the spacecraft as they come back on the frontside of the Moon.
Shortly after reacquiring, we expect Stu Roosa in Kitty Hawk to activate the television system there.
We have the lines up and we are prepared to receive the television in Mission Control and see the final closing and docking operation.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We read you loud and clear, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: Well, things just about nominal as they could be.
We had good TPI and midcourses of around 1.9 and 1.1 feet per second, Delta-V...Give you the exact numbers if you want them, but everything's just about nominal.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: We'd like the numbers for TPI.
transcript.speaker: CC

tokens: You're locked up on the steerable, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: Ed will give - Ed will give them to you.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: Okay, The numbers for TPI: Noun 81, plus 62.1, plus 0.1, plus 63.1; burned on time; and nulled PGNS to 00 plus 0.1.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: And say again about the steerable.
transcript.speaker: CC

tokens: I'm reading you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Could we have your TPI solution, please?
transcript.speaker: CC

tokens: Stu Roosa has the television active in the Command Module.
We got a brief picture here in the control center and then it dropped out again.
We'll stand by for getting a good lockup on the television.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: On the VHF - I mean a sextant only solution, I had a minus 67.4, plus 0.5, minus 69.2.
I did get the ranging to work when our comm got better after TPI.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Understand; you did get the VHF ranging going after TPI?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I was going to send you some TV, I had it on Standby and I went to Transmit and I could see the surface pretty good and all of a sudden it quit.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We can probably get it working again.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: How are you configured down there?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: I think that's a ground-commanded configuration problem.
And as soon as we're through dumping the backside tape, we'll give it back to you.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: What are you doing way down there, oh Fearless One?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Roosa's comment that the LM was way down there is a pretty good indication that they have not begun station keeping at this time and are still on the final approach.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I show you at 1.52 or something like that.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We'd like you to load the nominal LM weight and the DAP please, 5700.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I got a spot out over here where I - maybe I can do that, Bruce.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We'd expect the line of sight, braking maneuvers performed by the Lunar Module, to continue for about another 5 minutes before they're in the position to begin the station keeping.
And we will be standing by for completion of the data dump, dumping the data tapes, at which time we'll be able to bring up the television.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The line of sight through the COAS looks real good.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And we're beginning to get some indications of a television picture now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay Stu, we're getting a great TV signal now.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I don?t know if you can pick him up yet or not.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Okay, can you tell us roughly where he is in the monitor and grid coordinates?
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: He's right on the top of B and C.
On the line in between them it looks like, Bruce.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: I can't see that far over to the monitor.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: I'll look out the window here first.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We've got him at the left-hand edge of our picture about one-third of the way down from the top.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Okay, I believe I'll just have a few pictures of you here.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: You've lost a little weight since the last time I saw you.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: The range at this point is less then 1/10 of a mile.
The vehicles are closing at about 4 feet per second.
The poor quality of the television picture is due to a weak signal strength.
The communications engineer is looking into it to see what can be done, perhaps on the ground to improve the signal strength.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston, Antares is stationkeeping at about 100 feet.
Closing in a little more for the pictures of the service module and Command Module.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: We've got you on television, and It's looking beautiful.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Okay, anytime you're ready Al, you...
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Go ahead and turn it around, we'll photograph you.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: I'm going to turn the TV on here.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: ...Stu, looking at the ascent stage of the LM, it looks like there's something hanging loose from the bottom of it.
A piece of wire or insulation or anything.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I was going to wait until we got in a little closer.
Probably part of the separation plane, I'm sure.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Yes, we - we concur on that.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Let me turn the TV out here before I blast it into the Sun on this pitcharound, Bruce.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And, Stu, we'd like to confirm that you got the LM weight of 5700 pounds loaded in the DAP prior to the docking.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: The CSM Digital Autopilot is presently programmed to only handle the lone mothership.
After docking, the center of mass for the docked stack will change and hence the requirements for the DAP will be different, too.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, I see a smooth loop there.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: Because I dock and I'll go Free, and then I'll get all that squared away, but I'll load it in.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Okay, I shall do a loop, leader.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: There I was at 240,000 coming over the top.
transcript.speaker: Roosa.

tokens: That's our home away from home.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: Kitty Hawk is doing an extremely smooth loop.
We're sitting at 70 feet watching him go around.
All these streak patterns are radial and uniform.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: We would expect to get a television picture back once Stu Roosa has completed manuevering in the Command Module in the proper attitude for docking.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Man the old Earth went right through the COAS.
transcript.speaker: Roosa.

tokens: I'll come in a little closer.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: When you get a chance, on panel 16, would you check the ASA - AEA circuit breakers.
We've lost data from the AEA only.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Bruce wants the crew to check the circuit breakers supplying power to the AEA - the Abort Electronics Assembly, the computer section of the Abort Guidance System.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Yes, that ought to do it.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Houston, what that is trailing is a little bit of that foil...
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: ...on the bottom part of that tank area, there.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Looks like during separation, the foil...
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: The other side is down tight.
And the side you're looking at there is ripped out pretty badly.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And we got a real good TV picture.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Request Lo Bit Rate, Aft Omni.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: You want Lo Bit Rate, Aft Omni?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Let's see, I wouldn't come in much closer than that.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Aren't you going to do your roll?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Okay, why don't you Just stop it there.
I need to translate down and right, here.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And, Kitty Hawk, we show you...
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: You're on the Omnis and be advised I seem to have lost AGS, although I have no warning.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: I have a - Houston, I have the Logic On and would like a Go for Pyro Arm.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Kitty Hawk, Houston, You're Go for Pyro Arm.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We'd like to get the steerable up.
Pitch, 170; Yaw, plus 55; High Bit Rate and request that you and Kitty Hawk do not make contact until we establish good telemetry.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Say again the angles; let's check them.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: Loud and clear, Ed, and we got High Bit Rate.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: This has been your docking practice flight, Stu.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: There's a big sigh of relief being breathed around here.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: All over the world, there is.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: You ought to try it from up here.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: This world and out of this world, too.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Despite all the consternation and double-checking due to the troubles experienced during the first docking of the mission, everything has gone well on this occasion.
transcript.speaker: Commentary
